coredata.dc:title: In-vivo toxicological study of cysteamine modified carbon dots derived from Ruellia simplex on fruit fly for potential bioimaging
coredata.dc:creator: Pansari, Pratibha
Sahu, Surajita
Mishra, Monalisa
Gupta, Piyush Kumar
Durga, Geeta
coredata.dc:description: Modern medicine must shift its paradigm from conventional treatment strategies to theranostics to meet individuals' needs. Carbon dots, as a class of fluorescent materials, provide biocompatible and multifunctional solutions for a wide range of applications including clinical sensing, imaging, and drug delivery. Various studies have focused on their synthesis, photophysical properties, and innoxious nature but their potential biomedical applications for the in-vivo toxicity are yet limited. In this work, previously synthesized C-Dots (CDs) were utilized to determine their effect on the development of Drosophila melanogaster. Simultaneously, the genotoxic potential of CDs was evaluated on specific larval cell types that play important roles in immunological defence as well as growth and development. The gut organ toxicity of both CDs was studied using DAPI and DCFH-DA dyes wherein RS-CDs didn't show significant toxicity to the concentration 500 μg/mL whereas RS-Cys-CDs showed nuclear fragmentation and modest ROS (reactive oxygen species) production. Subsequently, trypan-blue assay, larvae crawling assay, touch sensitivity, adult phenotype, and survivability assay were performed. The trypan-blue assay shows the non-toxic nature of both CDs even at the concentration of 500 μg/mL. The high concentrations of RS-Cys-CD (500 μg/mL) were further associated with the alteration in touch behaviour and decrease in pupa hatching. In-vivo and ex-vivo fluorescence assessment of both the CDs exhibit bright fluorescence in green and red channels upon excitation at 485 and 577 nm respectively. The prominent imaging results from RS-Cys-CDs highlight the positive impact of surface modification.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Larval guts treated with 250 and 500 μg/mL concentrations of RS-CDs (Left Upper Panel) and RS-Cys-CDs (Left Lower Panel) along with negative and positive control stained with DCFH-DA and DAPI dyes. DAPI stained the micronuclei and DCFH-DA showed the ROS production in larval gut cells. Due to the high production of ROS, DNA breaks down in the gut cells and leads to the formation of multiple micronuclei, (B) The histogram indicates the no. Of micronuclei formation in control, positive control, and treated groups of RS-CDs and RS-Cys-CDs. (The statistical analysis was performed on Prism GraphPad software using unpaired Student's t-test with ∗P < 0.05, ∗∗P < 0.01, and ∗∗∗P < 0.001 level of significance).

Trypan blue staining of 3rd instar larvae of Drosophila melanogaster after exposure to various doses of RS-CDs and RS-Cys-CDs w.r.t. Control and positive control. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) Larval crawling assay: (a, e) Control, (b, f) Positive Control (c, d) RS-CDs, and (g, h) RS-Cys-CDs treated larval groups with 250 and 500 μg/mL concentrations respectively. (B) The histogram represents the larval crawling speed of the control, positive control (pc), and all treated groups. (The statistical analysis was performed on Prism GraphPad software using unpaired Student's t-test with ∗P < 0.05, ∗∗P < 0.01, and ∗∗∗P < 0.001 level of significance).

Touch sensitivity assay: The histogram represents the touch sensitivity score of control, positive control (pc) as well as both RS-CDs and RS-Cys-CDs at 250 and 500 μg/mL concentrations (The statistical analysis was performed on Prism GraphPad software using unpaired Student's t-test with ∗P < 0.05, ∗∗P < 0.01, and ∗∗∗P < 0.001 level of significance).

Phenotype screening of adult Drosophila melanogaster treated with RS-CDs and RS-Cys-CDs at 250 and 500 μg/mL concentrations. The following body parts like (A–F) Eye, (G–L) Bristles, and (M–R) Wing venation patterns were examined during the test.

The histogram represents % of pupal hatching score of control, positive control (pc) as well as both RS-CDs and RS-Cys-CDs at 250 and 500 μg/mL concentrations. (The statistical analysis was performed on Prism GraphPad software using unpaired Student's t-test with ∗P < 0.05, ∗∗P < 0.01, and ∗∗∗P < 0.001 level of significance).
Fluorescence imaging of larval gut: (a–d) In-vivo fluorescence images (e–h) Ex-vivo fluorescence images of larvae gut treated with RS-CDs and RS-Cys-CDs at 250 μg/mL concentration.
Comparative assessment of toxicity and fluorescence study of different Carbon Dots.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Modern medicine must shift its paradigm from conventional treatment strategies to theranostics to meet individuals' needs. Carbon dots, as a class of fluorescent materials, provide biocompatible and multifunctional solutions for a wide range of applications including clinical sensing, imaging, and drug delivery. Various studies have focused on their synthesis, photophysical properties, and innoxious nature but their potential biomedical applications for the in-vivo toxicity are yet limited. In this work, previously synthesized C-Dots (CDs) were utilized to determine their effect on the development of Drosophila melanogaster. Simultaneously, the genotoxic potential of CDs was evaluated on specific larval cell types that play important roles in immunological defence as well as growth and development. The gut organ toxicity of both CDs was studied using DAPI and DCFH-DA dyes wherein RS-CDs didn't show significant toxicity to the concentration 500 μg/mL whereas RS-Cys-CDs showed nuclear fragmentation and modest ROS (reactive oxygen species) production. Subsequently, trypan-blue assay, larvae crawling assay, touch sensitivity, adult phenotype, and survivability assay were performed. The trypan-blue assay shows the non-toxic nature of both CDs even at the concentration of 500 μg/mL. The high concentrations of RS-Cys-CD (500 μg/mL) were further associated with the alteration in touch behaviour and decrease in pupa hatching. In-vivo and ex-vivo fluorescence assessment of both the CDs exhibit bright fluorescence in green and red channels upon excitation at 485 and 577 nm respectively. The prominent imaging results from RS-Cys-CDs highlight the positive impact of surface modification.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

row.entry: Adenocarcinomic human alveolar basal epithelial cells

ce:section.ce:para: Since the 20th century, researchers have been using different dyes, stains, and contrast agents to label biomolecules within the cell and tissue. Fluorescent dyes such as fluorescein, rhodamine, green fluorescent protein (GFP), etc. Have been used for decades. However, their narrow emission, limited specificity, photobleaching, and toxicity restrain their biomedical applications (Xiao et al., 2021). The 21st century ushered in a new era of medical innovation and moved researchers from therapeutics to theranostics, which combines therapeutics and diagnostics to treat illnesses and deliver personalized care (Chen et al., 2017). The need for multifunctional agents led to the evolution of quantum dots which became the new member of the fluorescent family. Quantum dots (QDs) are fluorescent probes with efficient photostability, good quantum yield, tailored specificity, and excitation-dependent emission properties. Hence, the possibilities to use them as nanotheranostic agents are limitless (Bruns et al., 2017; Li et al., 2019) but the core toxicity of metal-based QDs hinders their application (Kuznetsov et al., 2023).

In the arena of QDs, Carbon dots (CDs/C-Dots) are a new class of fluorescent nanomaterials with a size <10 nm consisting of core and surface. The core of CDs comprises polycyclic carbon rings with sp2 and sp3 hybridization. In contrast, the surface of CDs possesses functional groups such as hydroxyl (–OH), aldehyde (–CHO), and carboxyl (–COOH) groups (J. J. Liu et al., 2020). However, the nature of both the core and functional groups can be tailored for specific applications by the judicious choice of the precursors such as the carbon source and modifying agents respectively (Zeng et al., 2021; Pansari et al., 2023). The exclusive physiochemical and photochemical properties of CDs such as excitation-dependent emission, good conductivity, high quantum yield, up-conversion fluorescence, multicolour wavelength-tuning in addition to aqueous solubility, excellent biocompatibility, and low toxicity make them suitable candidates for biomedical applications (Devi et al., 2019). The possible applications of CDs include nano theranostic agents for drug delivery, in-vivo fluorescence imaging, photothermal therapy, photodynamic therapy, etc (Jia et al., 2020; Chung et al., 2020).

Over the decade, carbon dots have been synthesized by using synthetic or green precursors via top-down and bottom-up approaches (Torres Landa et al., 2022). Researchers have opted for green approach-based synthesis as the resulting carbon dots from green synthesis are often more biocompatible, which makes them suitable for applications in biomedicine, including drug delivery, bioimaging, and biosensing. Moreover, the abundance of different metabolites can impart various functional groups onto the surface of carbon dots which enhance their properties and make them suitable for specific applications without the need for additional chemical modifications (Pansari et al., 2023).

In the past years, CDs were synthesized and modified by incorporating various green and synthetic precursors such as Sweet potato (Shen et al., 2017), o-phenylenediamine (OPD) (Bhattacharya et al., 2017), Ginkgo (Zhang et al., 2019), Catharanthus roseus leaves (Arumugham et al., 2020), Turmeric (Roy et al., 2022), Urea (Xie et al., 2022), for various biomedical application such as bioimaging, drug delivery, antibacterial, antioxidant activity and two-photon excitation imaging etc. The cell viability studies were carried out by typical MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) assay using various cell lines such as A549, MCF-7, HepG2, etc. The cellular level cytotoxicity assessment of CDs results in good viability and promises the further exploration of CDs in biomedical imaging and related applications. Moreover, only a few studies have examined the toxicity of CDs on animal models where beer-derived CDs were orally administrated to the mouse and even at the high dose (2 g·kg−1) no death was reported and all the major organs remained intact (Wang et al., 2019). In light of the limited data in the literature on the toxicity assessment of CDs, in-vivo experiments on CD toxicology may offer new opportunities for future research (Pagar et al., 2022).

Drosophila melanogaster (fruit fly) has extensively been used as a well-suited model for the toxicological study of nanomaterials. The fruit fly withholds several characteristics such as a short lifespan, rapid reproduction cycle, and relatively simple and well-characterized anatomy which allows researchers to investigate the effect of nanomaterial on cell-cycle regulation, signal transduction, apoptosis, gene, and pathway (Demir et al., 2022). These characteristics provided the researchers with an insight into the interaction of nanomaterials with living systems in a limited time. Furthermore, inherent simplicity and robust experimental techniques such as behavioural assay, imaging, toxicity assessment, genetic manipulation etc made them suitable in-vivo models for the evaluation of their effects on physiological processes (Affleck and Walker, 2019).

In our previous work, CDs were synthesized from green material using Ruellia simplex flower as the sole precursor (RS-CD) and followed by modification with cysteamine (RS-Cys-CD) (Pansari et al., 2024). The obtained CDs were systematically characterized by the different morphological, elemental, and optical characterization techniques. Herein, a comprehensive model to evaluate the impact of these CDs on in-vivo models is presented. We have incorporated Drosophila melanogaster as the test model organism for the evaluation of CD toxicity by behavioural, and chemical assay, along with fluorescence study. This work will provide new insight into the safety and functional properties of carbon dots by comprehensive evaluation of the in-vivo toxicity and fluorescent properties of two types of carbon dots (RS-CDs and RS-Cys-CDs) and highlight the differential impact of surface modification on toxicity and fluorescence imaging.

DAPI (4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole) (D9542), DCFH-DA (Diacetyldichlorofluorescein) (D6883), and Trypan blue (93595) were procured from Sigma-Aldrich, Merck, Germany. All the chemicals were of analytical grade and were used as received. The Milli-Q water was used to make the solutions throughout the experiments.

The imaging studies of Larve and its phenotype were done under the stereomicroscope (ACCU-SCOPE Inc., Commack, New York). The fluorescence imaging of the larvae gut was performed using confocal microscopy (Leica DMi8 Germany). The laser beam was excited in the range of 485–520 nm for the green channel and 577–703 nm for the red channel.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of RS-CDs and RS-Cys-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In the previously reported work, Ruellia simplex-derived CDs (RS-CD) and Ruellia simplex-derived cysteamine-modified CDs (RS-Cys-CD) were prepared (Pansari et al., 2024). In addition to synthesis, morphological, structural, and optical characterization studies have been executed to study the morphological, structural, and optical properties of CDs. The HR-TEM, XRD, RAMAN, XPS and FT-IR studies under morphological and structural characterization and optical characterization such as UV–Vis, PL, TRPL, and Q.Y., confirmed the synthesis of both CDs (Pansari et al., 2024).

1 mg/mL stock solution of CDs was prepared by mixing 5 mg CDs in 5 mL Milli-Q water. The prepared stock was further sonicated for 10 min to maintain a homogenized distribution of the CDs throughout the solution. The homogenous solution was further diluted as per the requirement of the experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In-vivo toxicity analysis on Drosophila melanogaster
Drosophila melanogaster strains and culture conditions
ce:section.ce:para: Oregon-R+ flies were used to conduct the study of CDs. The fly meal preparation was done using food 0.4 g agar-agar Type-I, 2 g sucrose, 2.5 g corn meal, and 1.25g yeast in 50 mL Milli-Q water (Priyadarsini et al., 2020). Following the food preparation, 150 μl of Propionic acid and 250 μl of Nipagin were added as an anti-fungal and anti-bacterial agent. The entire protocol took place under a laminar airflow cabinet (Siddiqui et al., 2020). Two concentrations i.e., 250 μg/mL and 500 μg/mL of RS-CDs and RS-Cys-CDs have been taken into the individual food vials. The flies were transferred into the food vial in a ratio of 5:3 for the female and male respectively. Flies were nurtured under the ideal conditions at an optimum temperature of 24 °C for 12 h of daylight/darkness cycle (Priyadarsini et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of the cytotoxic potential of RS-CDs and RS-Cys-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity assessment of RS-CDs and RS-Cys-CDs has been done on the 3rd instar larvae collected from individual food vials. The gut of larvae was dissected and co-stained with 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI), and dichloro-dihydro-fluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) to study the effect of reactive oxygen species (ROS) production induced by the RS-CDs and RS-Cys-CDs (Sahu and Mishra, 2020). These dissected larval guts of control and different concentrations were kept at 4 °C in 4% paraformaldehyde (PFA) overnight. The next day, PFA was removed and the guts were washed with 1 x PBS thrice for 5 min each. Following this, the guts were then rinsed twice with PBST for up to 10 min. In the due course, DAPI stain was added to the gut for 5 min followed by washing with 1 x PBS to prevent over-staining. Finally, the guts were mounted on a glass slide with 20% glycerol and imaged using a confocal microscope (Kumar et al., 2022).

Before the experiment, larvae were collected in 1 x PBS solution and properly washed to remove any food left over. Subsequently, larvae were transferred into the trypan blue assay and incubated for 30 min in a rocking shaker (ROCKYMAX) (Mishra and Panda, 2021). With the onset of time, larvae were washed with the 1 x PBS solution for 15 min to remove the extra stain, and the tissue damage was studied under the stereomicroscope (Mishra et al., 2017).

The touch sensitivity assay was studied on the third instar larvae. Five-third instar larvae from each concentration were collected and washed with the 1 x PBS solution to remove any food particles (Bag et al., 2020). The 2% agar plate was prepared and left to solidify. The larva's thoracic area was gently poked with a toothpick-mounted eyelash that serves as a mechanical stimulus. The larvae's response was recorded and scored as per the protocol (Mishra et al., 2017; Ekka et al., 2021).

The adult phenotypes were examined after the pupa hatched into the adults. For phenotype analysis 40 flies were taken from each concentration. Diethyl-ether was used to put the flies to anesthetize, and the eyes, thorax bristles, and wings were examined for any defects using a microscope (Anand et al., 2019).

The survivability study of Drosophila melanogaster was performed by counting pupa-hatching (Sabat et al., 2016; Cai et al., 2024). The third instar larvae were fed with RS-CDs and RS-Cys-CDs at concentrations of 250 μg/mL and 500 μg/mL for 45 min. Following the treatment, the larvae were transferred to freshly prepared food vials and allowed to hatch into adult flies. Fifteen larvae were used in three sets for each concentration along with positive control and control groups. After 7 days, the graph was plotted by the percentage of adults hatched from each concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In-vivo imaging of RS-CDs and RS-Cys-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As a means to study the in-vivo fluorescence of CDs, third-instar larvae were washed with 1 x PBS and fed with the RS-CDs and RS-Cys-CDs. Furthermore, both RS-CD and RS-Cys-CDs were taken into the individual Eppendorf tubes. Five larvae of every concentration were transferred to individual tubes and kept for 1 h (Padhan et al., 2022). Henceforth, the guts were dissected out and washed with the 1 x PBS solution. In the subsequent step, the gut was mounted on the glass slide to visualize it under the confocal microscope (Nayak et al., 2023a).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ex-vivo imaging of RS-CDs and RS-Cys-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Geared toward the ex-vivo fluorescence imaging of RS-CDs and RS-Cys-CDs, the late third instar larvae of Drosophila melanogaster were washed with 1 x PBS. The dissected gut of the third instar larvae was kept in a 4% PFA solution for 30 min. Following this, the first gut was washed with 1 x PBS thrice each for 5 min and stained with both CDs for 10 min (Parvin et al., 2013).

All the statistical data analysis was done using Prism GraphPad 9.0 software. The data is represented as mean ± standard error, and statistical analysis was performed using unpaired Student's t-test with ∗P < 0.05, ∗∗P < 0.01, and ∗∗∗P < 0.001 considered as the level of significance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and physicochemical characterization of RS-CDs and RS-Cys-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The green source-derived CDs were synthesized and functionalized to modulate their optical features to exploit them as nanoprobes for potential biomedical applications. Ruellia simplex flowers were taken as the precursor to synthesize RS-CDs via hydrothermal treatment followed by their modification with cysteamine to generate RS-Cys-CDs. The studies on morphological and optical properties of RS-CDs and RS-Cys-CDs were conducted which concluded that the structural and optical characteristics of these CDs can easily be tuned by surface functionalization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In-vivo toxicity analysis on fruit fly
Assessment of the cytotoxic potential of RS-CDs and RS-Cys-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were fed to the flies with the food to study their interaction with the epithelial cells of the intestine. DAPI is a fluorescent stain that binds strongly to adenine–thymine-rich regions in the DNA and enables the assessment of gross cell morphology (Rananaware et al., 2023). After the exposure of CDs with the DAPI, RS-CDs didn't show any significant damage to the nucleus at both concentrations whereas both concentrations of RS-Cys-CDs showed nuclear fragmentation, suggesting it to be slightly cytotoxic (Fig. 1 A and B). Furthermore, DCFH-DA was used to detect the reactive oxygen species in the cell, as the elevated level of ROS species due to the exposure of any nanomaterials leads to molecular damage (Demir, 2021). CDs treated larval gut were stained with DCFH-DA and it showed non-significant ROS generation at both concentrations of RS-CDs, whereas RS-Cys-CDs showed modest ROS production at the lower concentration. Nonetheless, the notable reduction in fluorescence intensity at the higher concentration of RS-Cys-CD (500 μg/mL) indicates the toxic nature of RS-Cys-CD through significant ROS generation (Fig. 1). The toxicity profile of both the CDs enables them to follow their journey one step further.

Trypan-blue assay was employed for the identification of cell death within the larval gut as it stains only the dead cells and makes it possible to distinguish between dead and alive cells (Kumar et al., 2022). Both the CDs don't show any trypan blue-positive stain at each 250 μg/mL and 500 μg/mL concentration (Fig. 2 ). This observation suggests that both the RS-CDs and RS-Cys-CDs are non-cytotoxic.

Different CD treatment groups were observed and they exhibited distinct crawling behavioural patterns. In the control and positive control study, larvae crawl with a speed of ∼7–8 cm/min with minimum pause and in a straight direction (Fig. 3 (A) a, e, and b, f). After the exposure to RS-CDs, at the 250 μg/mL concentration, a considerable difference was observed as the speed reduced to ∼3–4 cm/min (Fig. 3 (A) c). Whereas at the 500 μg/mL concentration of RS-CDs the crawling speed increases to ∼6–7 cm/min (Fig. 3 (A) d). In the case of RS-Cys-CDs, the crawling speed of both CDs remains around ∼6–7 cm/min (Fig. 3 (A) g, h). The results are further visualized as a histogram to exhibit the impact of CDs on larval crawling (Fig. 3 (B).

The touch sensitivity graph demonstrates that 3rd instar larvae exposed to different concentrations of CDs are more sensitive to touch than control and positive control larvae (Fig. 4 ) (Dhar et al., 2020; Kumar et al., 2022). Both RS-CDs and RS-Cys-CDs exhibited sensitivity to touch. While RS-CDs at both tested concentrations and RS-Cys-CDs at lower concentrations showed insignificant changes in sensitivity, RS-Cys-CDs at the higher concentration of 500 μg/mL exhibited significant alterations in touch behaviour. The change in touch behaviour upon ingestion of 500 μg/mL of RS-Cys-CD depicts the significantly toxic nature of RS-Cys-CDs.

Stress accumulation due to nanomaterial exposure during the cell development of larvae may affect the tissue and result in abnormal phenotypes (Kays et al., 2020; Kose et al., 2020). The eyes, bristles, and wings of adult flies were evaluated under the stereomicroscope. All the phenotype of treated flies in comparison with control flies was found to be normal (Fig. 5 A-R). The phenotypic observation of treated CDs suggested it to be completely non-toxic.

This assay measures the number of flies that successfully emerge from the pupal stage. Successful eclosion is a critical step in the life cycle and survivability of the organism, making the pupa eclosion assay a valuable component of a comprehensive survivability assessment. The graph (Fig. 6 ) shows that the percentage of pupae hatched in the positive control group is the same as in the control group. Additionally, at both concentrations of RS-CDs (250 μg/mL and 500 μg/mL) and the lower concentration of RS-Cys-CDs, the percentage of pupal hatching is equivalent. However, at the higher concentration of RS-Cys-CDs (500 μg/mL), a modest decrease in hatching was observed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In-vivo fluorescence study of RS-CDs and RS-Cys-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: It can be observed from the images that both the CDs showed fluorescence in red and green channels whereas the RS-Cys-CDs showed prominent results. There is significant green fluorescence from the larvae fed with CDs (Fig. 7 a–d), which demonstrates that CDs have excellent fluorescence emission properties, even after being swallowed by Drosophila melanogaster.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ex-vivo fluorescence study of RS-CDs and RS-Cys-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: During the ex-vivo study of fluorescence, the dissected gut showed meaningful fluorescence from the CDs. Both the CDs show fluorescence upon staining in green and red channels (Fig. 7e–h). Observing the images, it becomes evident that RS-CD as well as RS-Cys-CDs exhibit strong fluorescence. Specifically, surface-modified CDs (RS-Cys-CDs) demonstrate more prominent results as compared to RS-CDs.

In the race of remarkable efforts for the treatment of cancer, the core toxicity during treatment left room for improvement (Chrysostomou et al., 2023). The infancy of developing low-toxic materials for the development of “one for all” is the crucial undertaking of the future theranostic (Yu et al., 2022) which led to the development of CDs (Molaei, 2019). CDs exhibit excellent fluorescence properties such as excitation–dependent emission with tunable photoluminescence, and high quantum yields which can be modified by surface states, and synthesis methods. This tunability allows for multi-color imaging, which allows for distinguishing different cellular components or tracking multiple biological processes simultaneously. These optical properties of CDs made them wonder about human innovation for their futuristic theranostic application (Li et al., 2019). Despite the extensive literature on the design and development of CDs, the in-vivo model study focusing on the in-detailed toxicity assessment of CDs (Pagar et al., 2022) is scarce. The incorporation of these new carbon-based fluorescent materials may alter the activity or behaviour of a living organism which needs to be evaluated (Aljabali et al., 2023). Drosophila melanogaster is considered the model organism due to its genetic tractability, rapid life cycle, evolutionary conservation, cost-effectiveness, and ease of maintenance. This inherent simplicity renders it an ideal model system for uncovering the effect of toxins, drugs, imaging agents, etc. Herein, the effect of CDs after ingestion into the Drosophila melanogaster has been studied. CDs were administered through the food immediately after hatching from the eggs and the larva began feeding on the treated food. The cytotoxicity assessment of RS-CDs didn't show any significant toxicity even at higher concentrations; however, RS-Cys-CDs shows modest toxicity at higher concentrations (Fig. 1). Furthermore, the trypan blue assay didn't reveal any sign of cell death (Fig. 2). The touch sensitivity assay and larval crawling behaviour are used to evaluate whether the fluorophore is toxic to Drosophila melanogaster or not by affecting the touch or crawling ability of the larva (Demir et al., 2013; Srivastav et al., 2020). In the current study, the touch sensitivity and crawling speed of control, RS-CDs, and RS-Cys-CDs treatment groups did not show any significant difference, thus suggesting the non-toxic nature of RS-CDs administered along with Drosophila melanogaster food, whereas the higher concentration of RS-Cys-CDs shows modest toxicity (Figs. 3 and 4) (Chauhan et al., 2023; Sun et al., 2021). It appears that dietary CDs did not cause any significant neurodegenerative and locomotory changes in flies. The phenotypic assessment of Drosophila melanogaster sensory organs unveils that there is no organ impairment at any given concentration of CDs. Eye, bristles, and wings remained intact with the control (Fig. 5). Furthermore, during the survivability there was no decrease observed in the pupal hatching, nevertheless, a small decrease was observed in the case of RS-Cys-CD at the higher concentration (Fig. 6). These results conclude the modest toxic nature of RS-Cys-CD at higher concentration.

After the successful toxicological evaluation, these CDs geared their journey towards ex-vivo and in-vivo fluorescence imaging studies. The fluorescence imaging results indicate that newly synthesized RS-CDs and RS-Cys-CDs successfully behave as a contrast agent in the Drosophila melanogaster model during in-vivo and ex-vivo imaging. Multicolour in-vivo and ex-vivo, fluorescence imaging with CDs were validated as shown in (Fig. 7), which presents dark-field fluorescence images of larvae gut after staining with CDs.

With the object of in-vivo imaging, CDs were delivered to the larvae gut via oral delivery. Cysteamine-modified CDs (RS-Cys-CDs) showed comparatively brighter images in both in-vivo and ex-vivo assays (Fig. 7). This observation also concluded that the fluorescence intensity of C-Dots can be tuned by surface modification (Nayak et al., 2023b).

The comparative assessment of the toxicity and fluorescence properties of these carbon dots demonstrates that, even at higher concentrations, RS-CDs and RS-Cys-CDs exhibit better performance compared to those reported in the literature (Table 1 ). These results suggest that they are ideal candidates for further practical applications. These carbon-based based fluorescent agents hold significant promise for a wide range of futuristic biomedical applications such as super-resolution imaging, real-time in–vivo monitoring, multiphoton imaging, quantum sensing, etc. C-Dots can be further functionalized with different drugs, peptides, biomolecules etc and serve as both diagnostic and therapeutic agents. Their unique optical properties combined with excellent biocompatibility and versatility make them suitable for advanced bioimaging, theranostics, personalized medicine, and beyond (Wang et al., 2023).

In summary, the toxicity and fluorescence results indicate that both types of CDs i.e. RS-CDs and RS-Cys-CDs can be used as reliable imaging tools, as neither exhibits significant toxicity across all assays. However, the superior fluorescence performance of RS-Cys-CDs suggests that surface modification of C-Dots can enhance their properties. This work would lead to the rational design of the multifunctional CDs and it will be a step forward towards materials with ideal properties and functions for future research.
Any organizations for this work supported no funding.
This article does not contain any studies with human or animal subjects.
